name: Adam AS
description: First human and first prophet in Islam. Created by Allah from clay and given knowledge of all names. Sent to Earth as the first khalifah. Father of all humanity.

name: Idris AS (Enoch)
description: Prophet of high station mentioned in the Quran (19:56-57). Known for wisdom, piety, and early sciences. Raised to a high place by Allah.

name: Nuh AS (Noah)
description: Prophet who preached for 950 years. Built the Ark under Allah's command, saving believers and pairs of animals from the great flood. Ancestor of post-diluvian humanity.

name: Construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza
description: Built under Pharaoh Khufu (Cheops) of the 4th Dynasty. The Great Pyramid stood as the tallest man-made structure for 3,800 years. One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and the only one still largely intact. Built with approximately 2.3 million limestone blocks.

name: Construction of the Great Pyramid of Giza
description: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built as the tomb of Pharaoh Khufu. Standing 146 meters tall, it was the tallest man-made structure in the world for over 3,800 years. One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and the only one still standing.

name: Hud AS
description: Prophet sent to the ancient people of 'Ad in the Arabian Peninsula. They rejected his message and were destroyed by a fierce wind lasting seven nights and eight days.

name: Salih AS
description: Prophet sent to the Thamud people. Presented with the miraculous she-camel as a divine sign. When the Thamud slaughtered it, they were destroyed by a thunderbolt.

name: Ibrahim AS (Abraham)
description: Friend of Allah (Khalilullah) and father of monotheism. Broke his people's idols, was saved from fire, tested with the sacrifice of his son. Built the Kaaba with his son Ismail AS.

name: Lut AS (Lot)
description: Nephew of Ibrahim AS and prophet to the people of Sodom. Warned against immoral practices. The cities were destroyed by divine punishment while Lut and his believing family were saved.

name: Ismail AS (Ishmael)
description: Firstborn son of Ibrahim AS and Hajar. Left in the valley of Mecca as an infant; Zamzam water sprang for him. He and Ibrahim AS built the Kaaba. Ancestor of the Arab tribes and of Prophet Muhammad SAW.

name: Ishaq AS (Isaac)
description: Son of Ibrahim AS and Sarah, born miraculously in their old age. Father of Yaqub AS and grandfather of the twelve tribes of Israel.

name: Yaqub AS (Jacob/Israel)
description: Son of Ishaq AS and grandson of Ibrahim AS. Endured separation from his beloved son Yusuf for years. Father of twelve sons who became the twelve tribes of Israel.

name: Yusuf AS (Joseph)
description: Son of Yaqub AS. Sold into slavery by his brothers, imprisoned in Egypt, then rose to become the Aziz (minister) of Egypt. His story in Surah Yusuf is called the most beautiful of stories.

name: Shuaib AS (Jethro)
description: Prophet to the people of Midian and Aykah. Preached against dishonest trade and polytheism. His people were destroyed by a thunderstorm when they rejected him. Father-in-law of Musa AS.

name: Ayyub AS (Job)
description: Prophet famous for extraordinary patience during prolonged illness and loss. His supplication to Allah was answered and he was healed and blessed. A timeless model of endurance.

name: Dhul-Kifl AS
description: Prophet mentioned in the Quran (21:85-86, 38:48). Associated by many scholars with Ezekiel. Known for keeping his pledges and steadfast worship.

name: Harun AS (Aaron)
description: Brother and fellow prophet of Musa AS. Known for eloquence, assigned to help Musa preach to Pharaoh. Led the Israelites while Musa received the Torah on Sinai.

name: Musa AS (Moses)
description: One of the greatest prophets, called Kalimullah (he who spoke with Allah). Led the Israelites out of Egyptian slavery. Received the Torah on Mount Sinai. Performed miracles including parting the sea.

name: Trojan War
description: Legendary war between the Greeks (Achaeans) and the city of Troy (in modern Turkey). Immortalized by Homer's Iliad and Odyssey. Modern archaeology confirms a historical Bronze Age city at Hisarlik, Turkey, destroyed around this period.

name: Trojan War
description: The legendary war between a Greek coalition and the city of Troy, described in Homer's Iliad. According to tradition, sparked by the abduction of Helen. Archaeological evidence suggests a real conflict around this time at Hisarlik, Turkey.

name: Dawud AS (David)
description: Prophet and king of Israel. Slew Goliath (Jalut) as a youth. Given the Zabur (Psalms). Known for his melodious voice and deep worship. Iron was made soft for him by Allah.

name: Sulaiman AS (Solomon)
description: Son of Dawud AS and a great prophet-king. Given dominion over humans, jinn, wind, and birds. Built the great Temple in Jerusalem. Known for unmatched wisdom and a vast kingdom.

name: Ilyas AS (Elijah)
description: Prophet to the Israelites who challenged the worshippers of Baal on Mount Carmel. Taken up to heaven. Mentioned in Quran 6:85 and 37:123-130 as among the righteous.

name: Al-Yasa AS (Elisha)
description: Successor prophet to Ilyas AS. Performed numerous miracles. Mentioned in Quran 6:86 and 38:48 as among the best of creation.

name: Yunus AS (Jonah)
description: Prophet to the people of Nineveh. Left without permission and was swallowed by a whale. Made the famous supplication (Dua Yunus) inside the whale and was saved. His people repented and were spared.

name: Founding of Rome
description: Traditional founding date of Rome by Romulus. The city grew from a collection of villages on the Seven Hills into the center of one of history's greatest empires. The Roman Republic was established in 509 BCE.

name: Birth of Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama)
description: Siddhartha Gautama was born in Lumbini (modern Nepal). He attained enlightenment under the Bodhi tree and became the Buddha (Awakened One). Founded Buddhism, which became one of the world's great religions with approximately 500 million followers today.

name: Birth of the Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama)
description: Siddhartha Gautama, who became the Buddha (the Awakened One), was born in Lumbini (modern Nepal). He attained enlightenment under the Bodhi tree and founded Buddhism, one of the world's major religions, emphasizing the end of suffering through the Noble Eightfold Path.

name: Birth of Confucius
description: Chinese philosopher Confucius (Kong Qiu) laid the foundations of Confucianism — a system of ethical and social thought that deeply influenced Chinese civilization, government, and culture for 2,500 years.

name: Birth of Confucius
description: Confucius (Kong Qiu) was born in the State of Lu, China. His philosophy — Confucianism — emphasizing morality, family loyalty, ancestor veneration, and correct social relationships profoundly shaped East Asian civilization for over 2,500 years.

name: Founding of the Roman Republic
description: The Roman Kingdom was overthrown and the Roman Republic established with elected consuls replacing the king. The Republic's constitution, with its system of checks and balances, profoundly influenced later democratic governance including the United States Constitution.

name: Greco-Persian Wars
description: Series of conflicts between the Achaemenid Persian Empire and Greek city-states. Key battles: Marathon (-490), Thermopylae (-480), Salamis (-480). Greece's surprising victories preserved Hellenic civilization and shaped the Western world.

name: Construction of the Parthenon
description: Built atop the Acropolis in Athens under Pericles as a temple to Athena. A masterpiece of Classical Greek architecture and a symbol of democracy, rationalism, and artistic achievement of the Golden Age of Athens.

name: Peloponnesian War
description: Athens vs Sparta and their respective allies. A devastating 27-year war that ended Athenian hegemony. Plague killed a third of Athens. Spartan victory backed by Persian gold. Described by Thucydides in his landmark History.

name: Death and Trial of Socrates
description: The Athenian philosopher Socrates was tried and executed for impiety and corrupting youth. His pupil Plato recorded his final days in the dialogues. The trial became a foundational event in the philosophy of justice and free thought.

name: Death and Legacy of Socrates
description: The Athenian philosopher Socrates was tried and executed for impiety and corrupting youth. His student Plato recorded his ideas which became the foundation of Western philosophy. His method of questioning (Socratic method) remains central to critical inquiry.

name: Conquests of Alexander the Great
description: Alexander of Macedon conquered the Persian Achaemenid Empire, Egypt, and reached India in one of the most extraordinary military campaigns in history. Created a vast empire that spread Greek culture across the ancient world.

name: Founding of Alexandria
description: Alexander the Great founded Alexandria in Egypt. It became one of the greatest cities of the ancient world, home to the legendary Library of Alexandria — the largest repository of knowledge in antiquity. A center of Hellenistic culture and learning.

name: Founding of the Mauryan Empire
description: Chandragupta Maurya founded the first pan-Indian empire after Alexander's withdrawal. His grandson Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the bloody Kalinga War and spread Buddhist teachings across Asia.

name: Punic Wars (Rome vs Carthage)
description: Three wars between Rome and Carthage (modern Tunisia). Hannibal's invasion of Italy (218 BCE) was a near-fatal blow to Rome. Rome ultimately destroyed Carthage in 146 BCE. Established Rome as the dominant Mediterranean power.

name: Completion of the Great Wall of China
description: Qin Shi Huang unified the seven Warring States and linked earlier defensive walls into the Great Wall. Also created the first unified Chinese state, standardized weights, measures, and writing. One of the most ambitious construction projects in human history.

name: Zakariyya AS (Zechariah)
description: Prophet and guardian of Maryam (Mary). Despite extreme old age, prayed for a son and was given glad tidings of Yahya AS. Martyred according to tradition.

name: Yahya AS (John the Baptist)
description: Son of Zakariyya AS, born miraculously. Known for asceticism, righteousness, and being first to believe in Isa AS. Given wisdom from childhood. Martyred by Herod Antipas.

name: Isa AS (Jesus)
description: Prophet and Messenger of Allah, born miraculously to Maryam without a father. Performed miracles: healing the blind, raising the dead, speaking as an infant. Given the Injil. In Islam, he was raised to heaven and will return before the Day of Judgment.

name: Birth of Jesus of Nazareth (Isa AS)
description: Jesus was born in Bethlehem (modern Palestine). His teachings became the foundation of Christianity — the world's largest religion with approximately 2.4 billion followers. In Islam, he is the Prophet Isa AS, born miraculously to Maryam.

name: Roman-Jewish Wars
description: Three major revolts by Jews against Roman rule. The Great Revolt (66-73) ended with the destruction of the Second Temple (70 CE) and the Siege of Masada. The Bar Kokhba revolt (132-135) resulted in Jews being expelled from Judea.

name: Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA)
description: First wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW and first person to embrace Islam. Wealthy merchant who provided emotional and financial support. Mother of his children. Called the Mother of Believers.

name: Fatimah bint Asad (RA)
description: Mother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and an early convert who raised the Prophet after his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib died. The Prophet washed her body and lay in her grave to ease her passing. Called by him my mother.

name: Sawdah bint Zamah (RA)
description: Second wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW, married after Khadijah's death. Known for her warm and generous personality. Often gave her night's turn to Aisha RA out of love for the Prophet.

name: Abu Sufyan ibn Harb (RA)
description: Leader of Quraysh and longtime opponent of the Prophet. Embraced Islam at the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. Father of Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan. Lost his eye at the Battle of Taif.

name: Hassan ibn Thabit (RA)
description: The poet-companion of the Prophet. The Prophet gave him a pulpit from which to defend Islam with poetry. His satirical poems against the Quraysh were significant in the early Islamic period.

name: Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib (RA)
description: Uncle of Prophet Muhammad SAW and progenitor of the Abbasid Caliphate. Initially neutral, converted before the Conquest of Mecca. A respected figure in early Islamic history.

name: Sad ibn Ubadah (RA)
description: Chief of the Khazraj tribe in Medina. Among the Ansar who pledged at Aqabah. Considered for the caliphate after the Prophet's death but withdrew. Died in Syria, believed killed by jinn according to some narrations.

name: Salman al-Farisi (RA)
description: Persian companion who converted after a long spiritual quest through Christianity and Judaism. Suggested digging the trench at the Battle of Khandaq. The Prophet said: Salman is of us, the Ahl al-Bayt.

name: Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib (RA)
description: Uncle of Prophet Muhammad SAW and among the most courageous companions. Called Asadullah (Lion of Allah). Martyred at the Battle of Uhud. The Prophet declared him Sayyid al-Shuhada (Master of Martyrs).

name: Ammar ibn Yasir (RA)
description: Son of Yasir and Sumayya. The Prophet predicted he would be killed by the unjust party. Killed at the Battle of Siffin. Known for his unwavering faith and patience under torture in Mecca.

name: Abu Bakr al-Siddiq (RA)
description: Closest companion of Prophet Muhammad SAW and first Caliph of Islam (632-634). Called al-Siddiq (the truthful) for immediately believing in the Night Journey. Preserved the Quran's first compilation. Father of Aisha RA.

name: Khansa bint Amr (RA)
description: Famous Arab poetess who embraced Islam. Lost four sons at the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah and said: Praise be to Allah who honored me with their martyrdom. The Prophet praised her poetry and was moved by it.

name: Uthman ibn Affan (RA)
description: Third Caliph of Islam (644-656). Called Dhu al-Nurayn (possessor of two lights) for marrying two daughters of the Prophet. Standardised the Quran's written text (Mushaf Uthmani). Assassinated during his caliphate.

name: Asim ibn Thabit (RA)
description: Companion who vowed never to touch a polytheist, nor let a polytheist touch him. Martyred at al-Raji. The angels guarded his body from desecration, reportedly surrounded by a swarm of bees.

name: Zayd ibn al-Khattab (RA)
description: Elder brother of Umar ibn al-Khattab. Embraced Islam before his famous brother. Martyred at the Battle of Yamama fighting Musaylimah the false prophet. Umar would weep when reminded of him.

name: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. Among the first eight to embrace Islam. A successful merchant who donated generously to Islamic causes. Led the Muslims in prayer when the Prophet was ill.

name: Bilal ibn Rabah (RA)
description: First muezzin of Islam. An Abyssinian slave tortured by his master for accepting Islam, freed by Abu Bakr. His call to prayer (adhan) echoed in Mecca after its conquest. Settled in Syria after the Prophet's death.

name: Abdullah ibn Rawahah (RA)
description: Poet and companion from the Ansar. Among the earliest from Medina to pledge allegiance at Aqabah. Commanded the Islamic forces at Mutah after Zayd and Jafar fell. Martyred there in 629 CE.

name: Suhayb al-Rumi (RA)
description: Roman-raised companion who offered all his wealth to gain his freedom to emigrate to Medina. The Prophet received him saying: Your trade has been profitable, O Suhayb. A devoted and sincere Muslim.

name: Amir ibn Fuhayrah (RA)
description: Freed slave of Abu Bakr who helped the Prophet and Abu Bakr during their stay in the cave of Thawr during the Hijra by grazing his flock over their tracks. Martyred at al-Raji in 625 CE.

name: Abul-As ibn al-Rabih (RA)
description: Son-in-law of the Prophet, husband of Zaynab bint Muhammad. Initially opposed Islam but was captured at Badr. Returned to Mecca then embraced Islam sincerely and rejoined his wife Zaynab in Medina.

name: Sad ibn al-Rabih al-Ansari (RA)
description: Leader of the Khazraj tribe who hosted Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf when he first came to Medina. Known for extraordinary generosity — offered half his wealth and one of his wives. Martyred at Uhud.

name: Numan ibn Muqarrin (RA)
description: Commander who played a key role in the Battle of Nahavand (642 CE) against the Sasanian Empire. Also known as the conqueror of Nihawand. Martyred during that battle. A brilliant military strategist.

name: Usayd ibn Hudayr (RA)
description: Chief of the Aws tribe who embraced Islam through Musab ibn Umayr. The Prophet said the soul of Usayd and his family's lamps light up the sky. Known for his Quran recitation and piety.

name: Abu Rafi (RA)
description: Freed slave and servant of the Prophet. Trusted messenger and assistant. Participated in the assassination of Ka'b ibn al-Ashraf at the Prophet's direction. Highly trusted by the Prophet and his household.

name: Sumayya bint Khabbat (RA)
description: First martyr of Islam. Tortured mercilessly by Abu Jahl for refusing to recant her faith. Killed by Abu Jahl with a spear. Mother of Ammar ibn Yasir. Died before the Hijra to Medina.

name: Umm Ayman Barakah bint Thalabah (RA)
description: Abyssinian nursemaid who cared for the Prophet from childhood after his mother Aminah's death. The Prophet called her my mother after my own mother. She emigrated to both Abyssinia and Medina.

name: Hind bint Utbah (RA)
description: Wife of Abu Sufyan who initially bitterly opposed Islam. Responsible for desecrating Hamzah's body at Uhud. Embraced Islam at the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE and became a sincere Muslim thereafter.

name: Abu Dharr al-Ghifari (RA)
description: Among the earliest to embrace Islam. Known for his extreme asceticism, bluntness, and fierce opposition to inequality and accumulation of wealth. Exiled by Uthman to al-Rabadha where he died.

name: Zayd ibn Harithah (RA)
description: Adopted son of the Prophet and the only companion named in the Quran. The Prophet loved him deeply. He commanded the Muslim expedition to Mutah where he was martyred in 629 CE.

name: Aqil ibn Abi Talib (RA)
description: Elder brother of Ali ibn Abi Talib and cousin of the Prophet. Renowned for deep knowledge of Arab genealogy and history. Embraced Islam at the Conquest of Mecca.

name: Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. Called Amin al-Ummah (Trustee of the Nation) by the Prophet. Commander in the conquest of Syria. Died of plague in the Jordan Valley.

name: Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman (RA)
description: Companion entrusted with the Prophet's secrets, including the names of hypocrites. Called Sahib al-Sirr (keeper of secrets). Served as governor of al-Mada'in. Died shortly after Uthman's assassination.

name: Sa'd ibn Muadh (RA)
description: Chief of the Aws tribe in Medina. Embraced Islam through Musab ibn Umayr. At the Battle of Khandaq he was mortally wounded. The Prophet said the Throne of Allah shook at his death.

name: Abu Lubabah ibn Abd al-Mundhir (RA)
description: Companion of the Aws tribe. During the siege of Banu Qurayza, he warned them of their fate by a gesture. Overcome with guilt, he tied himself to a pillar in the mosque until Allah revealed his forgiveness.

name: Nusaybah bint Kab Umm Umarah (RA)
description: Warrior companion who personally defended the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud when most had fled. Fought in the Ridda wars and lost her hand at the Battle of Yamama. The Prophet said: Wherever I turned I saw her fighting.

name: Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA)
description: Second Caliph of Islam (634-644). Called al-Faruq (the one who distinguishes truth from falsehood). Expanded the Islamic state across Persia, Egypt, and Syria. Established the Islamic calendar. Assassinated by Abu Lulu.

name: Amr ibn al-As (RA)
description: Brilliant military strategist who conquered Egypt for Islam in 641 CE. Led the Islamic forces against the Byzantine Patriarch of Alexandria. Later served as governor of Egypt under Muawiyah.

name: Khalid ibn al-Walid (RA)
description: Called Sayf Allah al-Maslul (Drawn Sword of Allah) by the Prophet. Undefeated in over 100 battles. Commanded the Muslim forces against Byzantine and Sasanian armies. Conquered Syria and Iraq. Never lost a battle.

name: Jarir ibn Abdullah al-Bajali (RA)
description: Chief of the Bajilah tribe who embraced Islam in the 10th year of Hijra. The Prophet called him the Yusuf of this nation for his handsome appearance. Sent to destroy Dhul Khalasa, the idol of Yemen.

name: Safwan ibn Umayyah (RA)
description: Meccan leader who initially fled the Conquest of Mecca. Given safe conduct by the Prophet and later embraced Islam after witnessing Muslim virtue at the Battle of Hunayn. Became a sincere Muslim.

name: Khabbab ibn al-Aratt (RA)
description: Among the earliest converts to Islam. A blacksmith who suffered severe torture including having burning coals pressed against his back. One of the most persecuted companions in Mecca.

name: Abu Talha al-Ansari (RA)
description: Among the most devoted Ansar. His garden (Biraqa) was one of the finest in Medina and he donated it entirely for the sake of Allah. One of the most skilled archers among the companions.

name: Ikrimah ibn Abi Jahl (RA)
description: Son of Abu Jahl, the Prophet's fiercest enemy. Fled at the Conquest of Mecca but was brought back. Embraced Islam and became an ardent Muslim. Martyred at the Battle of Ajnadayn during the Syrian conquests.

name: Uthman ibn Mazun (RA)
description: Among the earliest converts to Islam and one of the first to emigrate to Abyssinia. Died in Medina in the second year of Hijra, becoming the first Muhajir to be buried at Baqi al-Gharqad.

name: Umm Habibah Ramlah bint Abi Sufyan (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW. Emigrated to Abyssinia; her first husband apostatized there. The Prophet proposed to her through the Negus of Abyssinia. Daughter of Abu Sufyan.

name: Zaynab bint Jahsh (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW and his cousin. Her marriage to the Prophet followed her divorce from Zayd ibn Harithah, clarifying Islamic law on adoption. Deeply charitable to the poor.

name: Jafar ibn Abi Talib (RA)
description: Cousin of Prophet Muhammad SAW. Led Muslim emigrants to Abyssinia and delivered a famous speech to the Negus about Islam. Martyred at the Battle of Mutah; both his arms were cut off before he fell.

name: Wahshi ibn Harb (RA)
description: Abyssinian slave who killed Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib at Uhud. Later embraced Islam and used the same spear to kill Musaylimah the false prophet at Yamama. He said: I killed the best of men and the worst.

name: Uqbah ibn Amir (RA)
description: Companion who excelled in archery, Quran recitation, and Islamic knowledge. The Prophet said he was gifted in fiqh. Governor of Egypt under Muawiyah. Narrated many hadiths on prayer and purification.

name: Abu Mas'ud al-Ansari (RA)
description: Companion from the Khazraj tribe of Medina. Narrated a famous hadith on the five prayers. Participated in the Battle of Badr. Later settled in Kufa and served as a scholar of the companions.

name: Shifa bint Abdullah (RA)
description: One of the few literate women in early Islam. Taught Hafsah bint Umar to read and write. Appointed by Umar ibn al-Khattab as a market supervisor (muhtasibah) in Medina — a remarkable position for any person.

name: Umm Waraqah bint Abdullah (RA)
description: Companion who compiled her own copy of the Quran. The Prophet visited her regularly and called her Shahidah (martyr) before her death. He appointed her as imam for her household. Martyred by her servants.

name: Khawlah bint al-Azwar (RA)
description: Female warrior companion who participated in the Battle of Ajnadayn disguised in armor. Fought to rescue her brother Dhirar from Byzantine captivity. Known as the greatest female warrior of early Islam.

name: Sa'id ibn Zayd (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. Brother-in-law of Umar ibn al-Khattab. Embraced Islam early and participated in most major expeditions. Known for piety and steadfastness.

name: Abdullah ibn Jahsh (RA)
description: First Muslim commander of a military expedition (Sariyyah). Cousin of the Prophet who led the Nakhla raid. Martyred at the Battle of Uhud alongside his brother Abd al-Rahman. Buried with Hamzah RA.

name: Maymunah bint al-Harith (RA)
description: Last wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW, married during the Umrah of 629 CE. Known for deep piety and worship. Narrated hadiths. Last surviving wife of the Prophet.

name: Talha ibn Ubaydullah (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. Shielded the Prophet at the Battle of Uhud, losing the use of his hand. Wealthy and extremely generous. Killed at the Battle of the Camel.

name: Zubayr ibn al-Awwam (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. First person to draw a sword in the way of Allah. Nephew of Khadijah RA and cousin of the Prophet. Killed at the Battle of the Camel.

name: Abdullah ibn Masud (RA)
description: Among the earliest converts and closest companions. First to publicly recite the Quran in Mecca. One of the greatest authorities on Quranic recitation and Islamic jurisprudence. Narrated over 848 hadiths.

name: Abu Darda (RA)
description: Known for knowledge, asceticism, and wisdom. One of the four who compiled the Quran in the Prophet's lifetime. Served as a judge in Damascus. Said: I seek refuge in Allah from a heart that does not tremble.

name: Ubayy ibn Kab (RA)
description: Master of the Quran. The Prophet told the companions to learn the Quran from four, including Ubayy. Known for his exceptional Quranic recitation and deep knowledge of Quranic sciences.

name: Musab ibn Umayr (RA)
description: First ambassador of Islam, sent to Medina before the Hijra to teach the Quran. The Prophet said he was the finest youth in Mecca. Martyred at Uhud carrying the Islamic standard. Buried in his travel robe.

name: Asma bint Abi Bakr (RA)
description: Daughter of Abu Bakr, sister of Aisha, and wife of Zubayr ibn al-Awwam. Called Dhat al-Nitaqayn (Lady of the Two Girdles) for tearing her belt to tie provisions for the Prophet's Hijra. Lived nearly 100 years.

name: Zaynab bint Khuzaymah (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW, known as Umm al-Masakin (Mother of the Poor) for her extraordinary generosity. Passed away only a few months after her marriage to the Prophet.

name: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas (RA)
description: One of the Ten Companions promised Paradise. Commander at the decisive Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, defeating the Sasanian Empire. First person to shoot an arrow in Allah's way. Founded the city of Kufa.

name: Miqdad ibn al-Aswad (RA)
description: Among the first to embrace Islam. At the Battle of Badr, declared the Muslims would fight even if they had to go to Barkul Ghimad. Known as one of only three companions who fought mounted at Badr.

name: Khuzaymah ibn Thabit (RA)
description: The companion whose single testimony was declared by the Prophet to be equal to two witnesses (Dhu al-Shahadatayn). Refused to fight at Siffin until Ammar ibn Yasir was killed, then fought and was martyred.

name: Umm Sulaym Rumaysa bint Milhan (RA)
description: Ansar companion known for her wisdom and courage. Her marriage dowry was her husband Anas ibn al-Nadr's conversion to Islam. Participated in battles nursing the wounded. Gave the Prophet musk as a gift.

name: Umm al-Fadl Lubabah bint al-Harith (RA)
description: Wife of Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib and second woman to embrace Islam after Khadijah, according to some narrations. Used a tent pole to strike Abu Lahab after he harmed her household. Mother of Abdullah ibn Abbas.

name: Umm Salamah Hind bint Abi Umayyah (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW, renowned for her wisdom and counsel. Widow of Abu Salamah who died after Uhud. One of the longest-lived wives of the Prophet. Narrated many important hadiths.

name: Thabit ibn Qays (RA)
description: Orator of the Ansar. The Prophet said of him: Thabit is among those of whom the Quran said do not raise their voices above the Prophet's. Martyred at the Battle of Yamama during the ridda wars.

name: Al-Qasim ibn Muhammad (RA)
description: First son of Prophet Muhammad SAW and Khadijah. Died in infancy. The Prophet took his kunya Abu al-Qasim from this son.

name: Zaynab bint Muhammad (RA)
description: Eldest daughter of Prophet Muhammad SAW and Khadijah. Married Abul-As ibn al-Rabih. Emigrated to Medina after her husband's capture at Badr. Her death deeply grieved the Prophet.

name: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari (RA)
description: The host of the Prophet upon his arrival in Medina. The Prophet stayed at his home for seven months. In old age, he participated in the siege of Constantinople and died there, buried outside the city walls.

name: Mughira ibn Shuba (RA)
description: Companion known for cleverness and political acumen. Participated in the Ridda wars, conquest of Iraq, and served as governor of Basra and Kufa under Umar and Muawiyah.

name: Abu Bakrah Nufay ibn al-Harith (RA)
description: Half-brother of Zayd ibn al-Harith. Lowered himself from a pulley at Taif to join the Muslims. The Prophet named him Abu Bakrah (father of the pulley). Settled in Basra and narrated many hadiths.

name: Julaybib (RA)
description: Companion of unknown origin and humble background whom the Prophet personally arranged to marry. Martyred in a raid; the Prophet found his body surrounded by seven slain enemies and said: This is of me and I am of him.

name: Rufaidah al-Aslamiyyah (RA)
description: First nurse and field medic in Islam. Set up a tent near the mosque in Medina to treat the wounded. The Prophet ordered wounded companions be taken to her tent after the Battle of Khandaq.

name: Ruqayyah bint Muhammad (RA)
description: Second daughter of Prophet Muhammad SAW. Married Uthman ibn Affan. Emigrated to Abyssinia, then Medina. Passed away the day of the Battle of Badr while Uthman stayed to care for her.

name: Abu Hurayrah (RA)
description: Most prolific narrator of hadiths — over 5,374 narrations. Embraced Islam in the year of Khaybar (628). Despite only 3 years with the Prophet, his prodigious memory preserved an enormous wealth of hadiths.

name: Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan (RA)
description: Son of Abu Sufyan. Became Muslim at the Conquest of Mecca. Served as a scribe of the Quran for the Prophet. Later governor of Syria and founder of the Umayyad Caliphate. First hereditary caliph of Islam.

name: Umm Kulthum bint Muhammad (RA)
description: Third daughter of Prophet Muhammad SAW. After Ruqayyah's death, she also married Uthman ibn Affan, earning him the title Dhu al-Nurayn (possessor of two lights).

name: Muadh ibn Jabal (RA)
description: A young Ansar companion known as the most learned in matters of halal and haram. The Prophet sent him to Yemen as a teacher and judge. Died of plague at age 36. Called the master of scholars.

name: Hafsah bint Umar (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW and daughter of Umar ibn al-Khattab. Entrusted with custody of the first written Quran compilation by Caliph Abu Bakr. Known for her intelligence and piety.

name: Kab ibn Malik (RA)
description: One of the three companions who did not join the Tabuk expedition. Their repentance and the subsequent revelation (9:118) is a famous story of divine forgiveness. Also a gifted poet who defended Islam.

name: Wathilah ibn al-Asqa (RA)
description: One of the last surviving companions who lived to approximately 100 years old. Embraced Islam shortly before the Battle of Tabuk. Settled in Damascus. Narrated hadiths until late in his life.

name: Qays ibn Saad (RA)
description: Son of Saad ibn Ubadah, chief of Khazraj. One of the most skilled Arab generals and administrators. Served Ali ibn Abi Talib loyally. Known for extraordinary generosity — debts of his household filled a register.

name: Hanzalah ibn Abi Amir (RA)
description: Companion martyred at the Battle of Uhud on his wedding night. Called Ghaseel al-Malaikah (one washed by the angels) because the angels washed his body, as the Prophet informed the companions.

name: Bara ibn Azib (RA)
description: Ansar companion from Medina. Participated in fifteen expeditions with the Prophet. Narrated important hadiths. Settled in Kufa and served as a scholar and transmitter of prophetic traditions.

name: Umm Kulthum bint Uqbah (RA)
description: First woman to emigrate alone from Mecca to Medina after the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. The treaty required Muslims to return Meccan refugees but the Prophet said it only mentioned men, so she stayed.

name: Juwayriyah bint al-Harith (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW from the Banu Mustaliq tribe. Her marriage led the Companions to free all captives from her tribe out of respect, and they embraced Islam.

name: Abu Qatadah al-Ansari (RA)
description: Called the horseman of the Prophet. The Prophet personally applied his saliva to his wound at Uhud. One of the most active military commanders among the Ansar. Settled in Kufa in his later years.

name: Abdullah ibn Abi Bakr (RA)
description: Son of Abu Bakr al-Siddiq who served as a secret messenger during the Hijra, bringing news of Mecca to the Prophet in the cave of Thawr. Mortally wounded at the Siege of Taif, he died months later.

name: Safiyyah bint Huyayy (RA)
description: Wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW and daughter of Huyayy ibn Akhtab, chief of Banu al-Nadir. The Prophet freed her and married her after the Battle of Khaybar. Known for her intelligence and dignity.

name: Abdullah ibn Umar (RA)
description: Son of Caliph Umar. One of the most prolific narrators — over 2,630 hadiths. Known for strictly following the Prophet's Sunnah in all matters. Declined political positions to focus on worship and knowledge.

name: Abdullah ibn Muhammad (Al-Tayyib) (RA)
description: Son of Prophet Muhammad SAW and Khadijah, also known as al-Tayyib and al-Tahir. Died in infancy.

name: Zayd ibn Thabit (RA)
description: Chief scribe of the Quran who learned Syriac and Hebrew at the Prophet's request. Led the compilation of the Quran under Abu Bakr and the standardization under Uthman. A supreme authority on Quranic text.

name: Abu Musa al-Ashari (RA)
description: Yemeni companion known for his melodious Quran recitation, which the Prophet compared to the instruments of the family of David. Governor of Basra and Kufa. Played a role in the arbitration at Siffin.

name: Sahl ibn Sad al-Saidi (RA)
description: One of the last surviving Ansar companions of Medina. Narrated many hadiths on the Prophet's character and habits. Lived to over 90 years and was a living link to the prophetic era late in the 1st century AH.

name: Anas ibn Malik (RA)
description: Personal servant of Prophet Muhammad SAW for 10 years. One of the most prolific narrators of hadiths — over 2,286 narrations. Lived to nearly 100 years. Among the last surviving companions.

name: Aisha bint Abi Bakr (RA)
description: Beloved wife of Prophet Muhammad SAW and daughter of Abu Bakr. Narrated over 2,210 hadiths. A leading Islamic jurist and scholar of her generation. Called Umm al-Muminin.

name: Usama ibn Zayd (RA)
description: Son of Zayd ibn Harithah. Called Hibb al-Nabi (the beloved of the Prophet). Appointed commander of an army at age 18. The Prophet declared his love for both Usama and his father.

name: Abu Said al-Khudri (RA)
description: Prolific narrator — over 1,170 hadiths. Participated in twelve expeditions. Among the Ansar who narrated the most hadiths. Well known for transmitting long hadiths on Islamic eschatology.

name: Zayd ibn al-Arqam (RA)
description: Companion who reported on the hypocrites' plot to the Prophet, causing revelation of Surah al-Munafiqun. Narrated important hadiths about the Ghadir Khumm sermon. Settled in Kufa.

name: Rafi ibn Khadij (RA)
description: Ansar companion who was too young for Badr and Uhud but participated in later expeditions. Narrated important hadiths on agriculture and contracts. A prolific narrator of the Ansar.

name: Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari (RA)
description: Prolific narrator of hadiths — over 1,540 narrations. Participated in numerous expeditions. Went blind in his old age. Among the last surviving companions. His hadiths are central to Islamic jurisprudence.

name: Imran ibn Husayn (RA)
description: Companion who heard the angels greeting him with salam. Suffered severe illness with patience. A major narrator of hadiths and settled in Basra where he taught Islamic sciences.

name: Maslama ibn Mukhallad (RA)
description: Companion born in the time of the Prophet. Governor of Egypt for twenty years under Muawiyah. Known for his wisdom and just administration. Built mosques and expanded Islamic infrastructure in Egypt.

name: Abdullah ibn Amr ibn al-As (RA)
description: Son of Amr ibn al-As. The only companion given permission by the Prophet to write down his hadiths in a collection called al-Sahifah al-Sadiqah. Known for deep piety and extensive fasting.

name: Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA)
description: Cousin of the Prophet, called Hibr al-Ummah (Scholar of the Nation) and Tarjuman al-Quran (Interpreter of the Quran). Narrated over 1,660 hadiths. Went blind in old age. Progenitor of the Abbasid line.

name: Numan ibn Bashir (RA)
description: Born in Medina, the first Ansari child born after the Hijra. His father brought him to the Prophet for blessings. Later served as governor of Kufa and Homs. Narrated hadiths on lawful and unlawful matters.

name: Umar ibn Abi Salamah (RA)
description: Stepson of Prophet Muhammad SAW and son of Umm Salamah and Abu Salamah. Raised in the Prophet's household. Later governed Bahrain under Ali ibn Abi Talib.

name: Battle of Uhud
description: Second major battle between Muslims and Quraysh. The Muslims initially advanced but a breach of orders by archers turned the tide. The Prophet was wounded. Hamzah ibn Abd al-Muttalib was martyred. A painful but instructive defeat.

name: Zaynab bint Ali (RA)
description: Daughter of Ali and Fatimah, granddaughter of the Prophet. Witnessed the tragedy of Karbala. Courageously confronted Yazid in his court and delivered powerful speeches exposing the injustice. Her shrine is venerated in Damascus.

name: Battle of Khandaq (The Trench)
description: Quraysh and allied tribes besieged Medina with 10,000 troops. Salman al-Farisi suggested digging a trench — unprecedented in Arabia. The confederates failed to cross and withdrew. A decisive defensive victory for the Muslims.

name: Battle of Khaybar
description: Muslim forces under the Prophet conquered the Jewish stronghold of Khaybar in the Hejaz. Ali ibn Abi Talib performed heroically, reportedly tearing off a fortress gate as a shield. The Jews were allowed to remain as tenant farmers.

name: Battle of Mutah
description: First Muslim engagement with the Byzantine Empire. A force of 3,000 Muslims faced a much larger Byzantine-Arab army in Jordan. Three commanders — Zayd ibn Harithah, Jafar ibn Abi Talib, Abdullah ibn Rawahah — were martyred before Khalid ibn al-Walid led the withdrawal.

name: Ibrahim ibn Muhammad (RA)
description: Son of Prophet Muhammad SAW and Mariyah al-Qibtiyyah. Died at 18 months, causing the Prophet great grief. A solar eclipse occurred on the day of his death.

name: Battle of Hunayn
description: Battle shortly after the Conquest of Mecca against the Hawazin and Thaqif tribes. Initial Muslim rout turned into victory. Quran 9:25-26 mentions how Allah's aid turned the tide. Large quantities of booty and captives were taken.

name: Abu Bakr al-Siddiq — 1st Caliph
description: First Caliph of Islam. Consolidated the Muslim community after the Prophet's death. Suppressed the Ridda (apostasy) wars, preserved the Quran in written form, and launched the Islamic conquests of Persia and Byzantium.

name: Battle of Yamama (Ridda Wars)
description: Major battle of the Ridda Wars against Musaylimah the false prophet. Khalid ibn al-Walid commanded. Many hafiz (Quran memorizers) were martyred here — a key reason Abu Bakr ordered the first Quran compilation.

name: Compilation of the Quran
description: After the Prophet's death (632 CE), Caliph Abu Bakr ordered the first written compilation of the Quran under Zayd ibn Thabit, fearing the loss of memorizers. Caliph Uthman later standardized the text (650 CE) into the Mushaf Uthmani used worldwide today.

name: Battle of Ullays
description: One of Khalid ibn al-Walid's early victories in the conquest of Iraq against the Sasanian-backed forces. Fought near the Euphrates. Khalid reportedly vowed to make the river run red, and the battle was exceptionally fierce.

name: Battle of Walaja
description: Khalid ibn al-Walid used a double-envelopment (pincer) maneuver against Sasanian forces in Iraq — a tactically sophisticated victory that opened the road to the Euphrates valley.

name: Umar ibn al-Khattab — 2nd Caliph
description: Second Caliph. Under his rule Islam expanded into Persia, Syria, Egypt, and Palestine. Introduced the Islamic Hijri calendar, established the diwan (state administration), and personally visited Jerusalem after its peaceful surrender.

name: Battle of Ajnadayn
description: First major engagement between Muslim forces and the Byzantine army in Palestine. Abu Ubaydah ibn al-Jarrah and Khalid commanded. A decisive Muslim victory that opened Palestine to the Islamic conquests.

name: Battle of The Bridge (al-Jisr)
description: A rare early Muslim defeat in Iraq. Muslim forces under Abu Ubayd al-Thaqafi were ambushed by Sasanian war elephants at a river crossing. Abu Ubayd was killed. The defeat temporarily halted the advance into Iraq.

name: Battle of Buwaib
description: Major Muslim victory over Sasanian forces in Iraq under al-Muthanna ibn Haritha. Helped recover Muslim morale after the defeat at the Bridge and resumed the advance into the Sasanian heartland.

name: Battle of Yarmuk
description: One of the most decisive battles in world history. Muslim forces under Khalid ibn al-Walid defeated the Byzantine army in modern Jordan/Syria. Opened Syria and Palestine permanently to Islamic rule. Byzantines never recovered these territories.

name: Islamic Conquest of Persia (Fall of Sasanian Empire)
description: Arab Muslim forces defeated the Sasanian Persian Empire following Qadisiyyah (636) and Nahavand (642). The last Sasanian Emperor Yazdegerd III was killed in 651. Persia gradually converted to Islam, contributing enormously to Islamic civilization.

name: Siege and Surrender of Jerusalem
description: Jerusalem surrendered peacefully to Caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab. The Patriarch Sophronius insisted that only the Caliph himself receive the keys. Umar visited the Church of the Holy Sepulchre but refused to pray inside to protect it from being converted.

name: Battle of al-Jalula
description: Muslim victory over Sasanian forces in Iraq, following al-Qadisiyyah. The Sasanian army was routed and retreated to their heartland in Persia. The road to Ctesiphon (al-Mada'in) was opened.

name: Islamic Conquest of Egypt
description: Amr ibn al-As led the Arab Muslim conquest of Byzantine Egypt. Alexandria fell in 641. Egypt became Muslim and Arabic became its language over the following centuries. The Islamic center shifted from Arabia to include Egypt permanently.

name: Uthman ibn Affan — 3rd Caliph
description: Third Caliph. Standardized the Quran's written text into the Uthmani Mushaf. Continued Islamic expansion into North Africa and Central Asia. Assassinated by rebels in Medina.

name: Ali ibn Abi Talib — 4th Caliph
description: Fourth Caliph and cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet. His caliphate was marked by the First Fitna (civil war) — the Battle of the Camel and Battle of Siffin. Assassinated in Kufa by Ibn Muljam. Revered by both Sunni and Shia Muslims.

name: Battle of the Camel
description: First civil war battle between Muslims. Caliph Ali ibn Abi Talib's forces defeated the coalition of Talha, Zubayr, and Aisha near Basra. Talha and Zubayr were killed. Aisha was escorted home by Ali. Named for Aisha's camel amid which fighting occurred.

name: Battle of Siffin
description: Inconclusive battle between Caliph Ali's forces and Muawiyah's Syrian army. When Ali's forces were winning, Muawiyah's troops raised Qurans on spears calling for arbitration — a famous moment of political manipulation. Led to arbitration and eventually the Kharijite schism.

name: Battle of Nahrawan
description: Ali ibn Abi Talib defeated the Kharijites — extremists who had seceded after accepting arbitration at Siffin. Though militarily victorious, the battle planted seeds of anti-Ali sentiment that led to his assassination.

name: Ali ibn Husayn Zayn al-Abidin (4th Imam)
description: Son of Husayn ibn Ali. Survived Karbala due to illness. Known for deep worship, charity, and his collection of supplications Al-Sahifa al-Sajjadiyya. Called Zayn al-Abidin — ornament of the worshippers.

name: Muawiyah I ibn Abi Sufyan
description: Founder of the Umayyad Caliphate. Moved the capital from Medina to Damascus. Built the first Islamic navy. An astute administrator and diplomat who stabilized the Islamic state after the First Fitna.

name: Sukaynah bint Husayn (RA)
description: Granddaughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and great-granddaughter of Prophet Muhammad SAW. Present at Karbala as a child. Known for her eloquence, beauty, and piety. A leading figure of the Ahl al-Bayt after Karbala.

name: Muhammad al-Baqir (5th Imam)
description: Son of Zayn al-Abidin. Fifth Imam for Twelver Shia. Called al-Baqir (one who splits open knowledge). Established important jurisprudential schools and is revered by both Sunni and Shia scholars.

name: Yazid I ibn Muawiyah
description: Second Umayyad Caliph. His caliphate was marked by the tragic massacre of Husayn ibn Ali at Karbala (680) and the Battle of al-Harrah against Medina. His reign caused lasting divisions in the Muslim world.

name: Battle of Karbala
description: The most pivotal and tragic event in Islamic history after the Prophet's death. Husayn ibn Ali, grandson of the Prophet, and 72 companions were killed by Yazid's Umayyad forces at Karbala, Iraq. Deeply split Islam into Sunni and Shia. Commemorated annually on Ashura.

name: Muawiyah II ibn Yazid
description: Third Umayyad Caliph. Ruled for only a few months. Voluntarily abdicated citing unworthiness, and died shortly after. His brief reign led to the Second Fitna and the rise of Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr's rival caliphate.

name: Battle of al-Harrah
description: Umayyad forces under Muslim ibn Uqbah attacked Medina after it rebelled against Yazid I. The city was sacked and the citizens subjected to three days of pillage. One of the most condemned events in early Islamic history.

name: Marwan I ibn al-Hakam
description: Fourth Umayyad Caliph. Restored Umayyad power against Ibn al-Zubayr's rival caliphate. Won the Battle of Marj Rahit (684). Died after only nine months, possibly murdered by his wife.

name: Battle of Marj Rahit
description: Marwan I defeated the Qaysi Arabs supporting Ibn al-Zubayr's rival caliphate. Established Marwanid Umayyad control over Syria. Deepened the tribal split between Qays (northern) and Yaman (southern) Arabs that would plague the Umayyads.

name: Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan
description: Fifth Umayyad Caliph who reunified the caliphate after defeating Ibn al-Zubayr. Arabized the state administration, introduced the first Islamic gold dinar coin, and built the Dome of the Rock in Jerusalem.

name: Jafar al-Sadiq (6th Imam)
description: Son of Muhammad al-Baqir. Sixth Imam for Twelver Shia and a pivotal figure in Islamic jurisprudence. Imam Abu Hanifa and Imam Malik both studied under him. Contributed to theology, chemistry, and Islamic law.

name: Al-Walid I ibn Abd al-Malik
description: Sixth Umayyad Caliph. The Islamic state reached its greatest territorial extent under his rule. Oversaw the conquest of Spain (711), Sindh (711), and Transoxiana (712). Built the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus.

name: Battle of Debal — Conquest of Sindh
description: Muhammad ibn Qasim's forces landed at Debal (near modern Karachi) and began the Muslim conquest of the Sindh region. Led to the first permanent Islamic state in the Indian subcontinent.

name: Battle of Guadalete — Conquest of Spain
description: Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed from Morocco with 7,000 troops and defeated the Visigothic King Roderic at the Battle of Guadalete. Opened the Iberian Peninsula to the Muslim conquests. Within years, nearly all of Spain was under Muslim control.

name: Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik
description: Seventh Umayyad Caliph who launched the great siege of Constantinople (716-717). The siege failed. Known for his efforts at administrative reform and briefly embracing the advice of the righteous Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz.

name: Umar II ibn Abd al-Aziz
description: Eighth Umayyad Caliph, widely considered the most just and pious of the Umayyad rulers. Called the fifth Rightly Guided Caliph by many. Reduced taxes, opposed oppression, and focused on Islamic reform. Died young, possibly poisoned.

name: Yazid II ibn Abd al-Malik
description: Ninth Umayyad Caliph. Faced the Yazid ibn al-Muhallab rebellion in Iraq. Known for his love of music and poetry, contrasting with his predecessor Umar II. His reign saw Kharijite uprisings.

name: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik
description: Tenth Umayyad Caliph and one of the longest-ruling. Known for efficient administration and fiscal reform. Oversaw the Battle of Tours/Poitiers (732) where Charles Martel halted Muslim expansion in France.

name: Battle of Tours (Battle of Poitiers)
description: Charles Martel's Frankish forces halted the northernmost Muslim advance into Europe near Poitiers, France. The Muslim commander Abd al-Rahman al-Ghafiqi was killed. Considered by European historians as a turning point that preserved Christian Europe.

name: Al-Walid II ibn Yazid
description: Eleventh Umayyad Caliph who came to power as a dissolute and controversial ruler. His behavior alienated religious scholars and military leaders. Killed by opponents who set his palace on fire.

name: Yazid III ibn al-Walid
description: Twelfth Umayyad Caliph who came to power by overthrowing al-Walid II. Reigned for only six months before dying. Called Yazid al-Naqis (the Deficient) for reducing soldiers' pay.

name: Ibrahim ibn al-Walid
description: Thirteenth Umayyad Caliph who briefly held power for two months before Marwan II marched on Syria. Surrendered and was later executed.

name: Marwan II ibn Muhammad
description: Last Umayyad Caliph of Damascus. A capable general who worked to reunify the caliphate. Defeated and pursued by the Abbasids after the Battle of the Zab (750). Killed in Egypt, ending the Umayyad dynasty in the east.

name: Musa al-Kadhim (7th Imam)
description: Son of Jafar al-Sadiq. Seventh Imam for Twelver Shia. Called al-Kadhim (the one who suppresses anger). Imprisoned by Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid and died in captivity.

name: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah — 1st Abbasid Caliph
description: First Abbasid Caliph. Led the Abbasid Revolution that overthrew the Umayyads in 750 CE. Called al-Saffah (the Blood-Shedder) for the massacre of Umayyad princes. Moved the capital to the newly founded city of al-Hashimiyya.

name: Battle of the Zab
description: Abbasid forces under Abdullah ibn Ali defeated the last Umayyad Caliph Marwan II on the Greater Zab river in Iraq. Marwan II fled and was killed in Egypt. Ended the Umayyad Caliphate and established the Abbasid dynasty.

name: Battle of Talas
description: Arab Abbasid forces defeated the Tang Chinese army in Central Asia near the Talas River (modern Kyrgyzstan). Determined that Central Asia would remain Muslim, not Chinese. Chinese papermakers captured in the battle brought the technology of papermaking to the Islamic world.

name: Al-Mansur — 2nd Abbasid Caliph
description: Second Abbasid Caliph and true architect of the Abbasid state. Founded Baghdad (City of Peace) in 762 CE as the new capital. Consolidated Abbasid power and patronized scholarship and translation.

name: Abd al-Rahman I — Amir of Cordoba
description: Escaped the Abbasid massacre of the Umayyads and established an independent emirate in al-Andalus (Spain). Called al-Dakhil (the Newcomer). Founded the Great Mosque of Cordoba. United Muslim Spain under his rule.

name: Founding of Baghdad
description: Abbasid Caliph al-Mansur founded the City of Peace (Madinat al-Salam) on the Tigris River. Baghdad became the largest city in the world, a center of the Islamic Golden Age, and home to the famous House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikmah).

name: Ali al-Ridha (8th Imam)
description: Son of Musa al-Kadhim. Eighth Imam for Twelver Shia. Named heir apparent by Abbasid caliph al-Ma'mun. Died in Khorasan, possibly by poisoning. His shrine in Mashhad, Iran is among the holiest Shia sites.

name: Al-Mahdi — 3rd Abbasid Caliph
description: Third Abbasid Caliph who sought to reconcile with the Alids and promoted trade. His reign was peaceful and prosperous. Father of Harun al-Rashid. Combated Manichean and Zindiq heresies.

name: Al-Hadi — 4th Abbasid Caliph
description: Fourth Abbasid Caliph who reigned for only one year. Faced the Husaynid revolt at Fakhkh (786). Died suddenly, possibly poisoned, leading to his brother Harun al-Rashid ascending the throne.

name: Harun al-Rashid — 5th Abbasid Caliph
description: Fifth Abbasid Caliph and one of the most famous rulers in world history. His reign was the pinnacle of the Abbasid Golden Age. Exchanged embassies with Charlemagne. The tales of One Thousand and One Nights are set in his court.

name: Hisham I — Amir of Cordoba
description: Son of Abd al-Rahman I. Promoted Maliki jurisprudence in al-Andalus. Conducted several raids (ghazawat) into northern Spain. Known for piety and justice in his administration.

name: Al-Hakam I — Amir of Cordoba
description: Amir of Cordoba who faced several major rebellions. Brutally suppressed the Suburb Revolt in Cordoba (818) and exiled thousands. Expanded the Great Mosque of Cordoba.

name: Al-Amin — 6th Abbasid Caliph
description: Sixth Abbasid Caliph who fought a civil war against his brother al-Ma'mun. Besieged in Baghdad, he was captured and executed, ending the first major Abbasid civil war.

name: Muhammad al-Jawad (9th Imam)
description: Son of Ali al-Ridha. Ninth Imam for Twelver Shia, became Imam at a young age. Called al-Jawad (the generous). Died young under Abbasid watch, believed by Shia to have been poisoned.

name: Samanid Dynasty
description: First major Persian Muslim dynasty to gain independence from the Abbasid Caliphate. Based in Transoxiana (Central Asia). Patronized the Persian language revival and great scholars including Avicenna (Ibn Sina).

name: Abd al-Rahman II — Amir of Cordoba
description: Amir who reorganized the administration of al-Andalus on Abbasid lines. A patron of the arts who invited the musician Ziryab from Baghdad. Expanded and enriched Cordoba as a cultural center.

name: Ali al-Hadi (10th Imam)
description: Son of Muhammad al-Jawad. Tenth Imam for Twelver Shia. Placed under house arrest in Samarra by Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil. His shrine is in Samarra, Iraq alongside his son al-Askari.

name: Islamic Golden Age and House of Wisdom
description: The Abbasid-era House of Wisdom in Baghdad translated Greek, Persian, and Indian texts into Arabic and produced original advances in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, chemistry, and philosophy. The greatest flourishing of human knowledge since ancient Greece.

name: Al-Mutasim — 8th Abbasid Caliph
description: Eighth Abbasid Caliph who created the powerful Turkish slave army (ghulam). Moved the capital to Samarra to house the Turkish troops. Defeated the Byzantine Emperor Theophilos and sacked Amorium.

name: Al-Wathiq — 9th Abbasid Caliph
description: Ninth Abbasid Caliph who continued his father al-Mutasim's policies. Patronized scholars and poets. Faced Bedouin and Byzantine frontier conflicts. Power was increasingly dominated by Turkish commanders.

name: Hasan al-Askari (11th Imam)
description: Son of Ali al-Hadi. Eleventh Imam for Twelver Shia. Lived under Abbasid surveillance in Samarra. Died young. His shrine in Samarra was destroyed in 2006 and later rebuilt.

name: Al-Mutawakkil — 10th Abbasid Caliph
description: Tenth Abbasid Caliph who reversed al-Ma'mun's Mutazilite policies and championed traditional Sunni Islam. Destroyed the shrine of Husayn at Karbala. Assassinated by his own Turkish guards and son.

name: Muhammad I — Amir of Cordoba
description: Son of Abd al-Rahman II. Faced significant Mozarab (Christian) unrest and the martyrs of Cordoba movement. Maintained Muslim political authority across al-Andalus.

name: Al-Muntasir — 11th Abbasid Caliph
description: Eleventh Abbasid Caliph who came to power complicit in his father al-Mutawakkil's murder. Reigned only six months before dying — possibly poisoned by Turkish commanders.

name: Al-Mustain — 12th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twelfth Abbasid Caliph entirely under the control of Turkish military commanders. Civil war broke out in Baghdad between rival Turkish factions. Deposed, then beheaded by order of the next caliph.

name: Al-Mutazz — 13th Abbasid Caliph
description: Thirteenth Abbasid Caliph manipulated by Turkish commanders. Attempted to reduce their power but failed. Tortured and killed by his own Turkish guard in one of the most degrading episodes in Abbasid history.

name: Muhammad al-Mahdi (12th Imam)
description: Son of Hasan al-Askari. Twelfth Imam for Twelver Shia. Believed to be in Occultation (ghaybah) since 874 CE, expected to return at the end of times to establish justice. Called al-Mahdi al-Muntazar — the Awaited One.

name: Al-Muhtadi — 14th Abbasid Caliph
description: Fourteenth Abbasid Caliph who tried to be a second Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz — austere and reform-minded. Attempted to curb Turkish power. Killed by Turkish soldiers after only one year.

name: Al-Mutamid — 15th Abbasid Caliph
description: Fifteenth Abbasid Caliph. Actual power held by his brother al-Muwaffaq, who defeated the great Zanj Rebellion. The longest-reigning Abbasid caliph of the Turkish-dominated period.

name: Al-Mundhir — Amir of Cordoba
description: Brief-reigning Amir who died fighting the rebel Umar ibn Hafsun. His short rule was entirely dominated by the struggle against the major Muwallad (native convert) rebellion in al-Andalus.

name: Abdullah ibn Muhammad — Amir of Cordoba
description: Amir who faced widespread rebellions from Muwallad and Christian lords. Lost control of much of al-Andalus during his reign but maintained Cordoba. Grandfather of the great Abd al-Rahman III.

name: Al-Mutadid — 16th Abbasid Caliph
description: Sixteenth Abbasid Caliph who restored some caliphal authority. An energetic ruler who reduced Turkish influence and recaptured some lost territories. Moved the capital back toward Baghdad.

name: Ismail I al-Samani — Founder Samanid Dynasty
description: Founder of the independent Samanid state. Defeated the Saffarids in 900 CE and was recognized by the Abbasid caliph as governor of Transoxiana and Khorasan. A just ruler who codified Persian administrative practices. His mausoleum in Bukhara is a masterpiece of early Islamic architecture.

name: Al-Muktafi — 17th Abbasid Caliph
description: Seventeenth Abbasid Caliph. Continued his father al-Mutadid's policies and successfully suppressed the Qaramita threat. His brief reign was relatively stable and prosperous.

name: Ahmad II ibn Ismail — Samanid Emir
description: Son of Ismail I. Consolidated Samanid power. Murdered by his own Turkish slave guards. His death began a period of instability.

name: Al-Muqtadir — 18th Abbasid Caliph
description: Eighteenth Abbasid Caliph and one of the longest reigning. His court received the famous embassy of Ibn Fadlan. Power was wielded by viziers and his mother. Killed during a revolt in 932.

name: Abd al-Rahman III — Caliph of Cordoba
description: Greatest ruler of Muslim Spain. Reunified al-Andalus, defeated rebellions, and in 929 CE proclaimed himself Caliph and Commander of the Faithful, challenging both the Abbasids and Fatimids. Made Cordoba the most cultured city in Europe.

name: Nasr II ibn Ahmad — Samanid Emir
description: The greatest Samanid emir, ruling for nearly 30 years. His court in Bukhara was a great center of Persian learning. The poets Rudaki and Daqiqi flourished here. Showed sympathy for Ismaili ideas which caused a brief revolt.

name: Al-Qahir — 19th Abbasid Caliph
description: Nineteenth Abbasid Caliph who seized power brutally. Was himself blinded and deposed by Turkish commanders after only two years. Later died in abject poverty.

name: Al-Radi — 20th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twentieth Abbasid Caliph, considered the last caliph to exercise real political authority. After him, caliphs became largely ceremonial figures while the Buyids and others held real power.

name: Al-Qaim — 2nd Fatimid Caliph
description: Second Fatimid Caliph who defended the dynasty in North Africa against the Kharijite Kharidjite Abu Yazid rebellion. Survived prolonged siege and repelled the rebels, though the rebellion was ultimately crushed by his son.

name: Al-Muttaqi — 21st Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-first Abbasid Caliph who spent his reign fleeing from various warlords. Finally blinded and deposed by the Hamdanid Nasir al-Dawla.

name: Nuh I ibn Nasr — Samanid Emir
description: Son of Nasr II. Faced increasing Turkish military influence in his court. The real power began shifting to Turkish military commanders during his reign.

name: Al-Mustakfi — 22nd Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-second Abbasid Caliph who invited the Buyids (Shia Persians) into Baghdad to counter his enemies. The Buyids entered in 945 and reduced the caliphate to a purely ceremonial institution. He was blinded by the Buyids.

name: Al-Muti — 23rd Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-third Abbasid Caliph. Reigned entirely under Buyid domination. The caliphate retained only religious prestige. He finally abdicated due to ill health after 28 years — the longest formal reign of the later Abbasids.

name: Al-Mansur — 3rd Fatimid Caliph
description: Third Fatimid Caliph who finally crushed the Abu Yazid rebellion. Founded the city of Mansuriyya as his capital in Tunisia. Stabilized Fatimid rule in North Africa.

name: Al-Muizz — 4th Fatimid Caliph
description: Fourth Fatimid Caliph who conquered Egypt in 969 CE and founded Cairo (al-Qahira) as the new Fatimid capital. A brilliant ruler who extended Fatimid power across North Africa and into the Levant. Founded al-Azhar mosque and university.

name: Abd al-Malik I — Samanid Emir
description: Son of Nuh I. Died young after falling from his horse during a polo match. His brief reign maintained Samanid stability.

name: Al-Hakam II — Caliph of Cordoba
description: Son of Abd al-Rahman III. A great scholar-caliph who assembled a library of 400,000 books. Extended the Great Mosque of Cordoba and promoted learning. The golden age of Cordoba reached its peak under his rule.

name: Mansur I — Samanid Emir
description: Long-reigning Samanid emir. The physician Ibn Sina (Avicenna) was born in his domains during this period. The Samanid state remained a major center of Persian Islamic culture.

name: Al-Tai — 24th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-fourth Abbasid Caliph under Buyid control. Deposed and imprisoned by the Buyid Bahaa al-Dawla after 17 years.

name: Al-Aziz — 5th Fatimid Caliph
description: Fifth Fatimid Caliph who consolidated Fatimid rule in Egypt and expanded trade. Known for his tolerance toward Jews and Christians. Built the great al-Azhar mosque and promoted learning in Cairo.

name: Hisham II — Caliph of Cordoba
description: Son of al-Hakam II, became caliph as a child. Real power held by the chamberlain Al-Mansur ibn Abi Amir, who led devastating raids into Christian Spain. The caliphate began to fragment after al-Mansur's death in 1002.

name: Nuh II ibn Mansur — Samanid Emir
description: Samanid emir under whom the dynasty began its fatal decline. Employed the Ghaznavid military commander Sebuktigin and his son Mahmud, who gradually became more powerful than their nominal masters.

name: Ghaznavid Sultanate
description: Turkic dynasty founded by Sebuktigin, centered in Ghazni (modern Afghanistan). Under Mahmud of Ghazni it became a major power. At its height controlled Khorasan, Afghanistan, and northwest India.

name: Sebuktigin — Founder Ghaznavid Dynasty
description: Former Samanid slave commander who founded the Ghaznavid dynasty based in Ghazni, Afghanistan. Extended control over Khorasan and raided northwestern India. Father of the great Mahmud of Ghazni.

name: Al-Qadir — 25th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-fifth Abbasid Caliph who reasserted caliphal religious authority despite Buyid political control. Issued the Qadiri Creed (1017-1018) asserting traditional Sunni doctrine against Mutazilism and Shia tendencies.

name: Ismail of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Sebuktigin who briefly succeeded his father before being overthrown by his more capable brother Mahmud. His reign lasted less than a year.

name: Mansur II ibn Nuh — Samanid Emir
description: Later Samanid emir who lost Transoxiana to the Karakhanids (999 CE). The Karakhanid conquest was a catastrophic blow and effectively ended Samanid power north of the Oxus.

name: Abd al-Malik II — Samanid Emir
description: Brief Samanid emir who could not reverse the collapse. The Ghaznavids controlled Khorasan and the Karakhanids held Transoxiana. The Samanid state was squeezed out of existence.

name: Ismail II al-Muntasir — Last Samanid Emir
description: Last Samanid emir who continued a guerrilla resistance against the Ghaznavids and Karakhanids. Wandered homeless with a band of followers. Finally killed in 1005. End of the Samanid dynasty — the first great Persian Muslim dynasty.

name: Muhammad II al-Mahdi — Caliph of Cordoba
description: Umayyad prince who seized the caliphate from Hisham II. His brief reign marked the beginning of the Fitna of al-Andalus, a catastrophic civil war that broke the Umayyad caliphate apart.

name: Sulayman al-Mustain — Caliph of Cordoba
description: Umayyad rival who contested the caliphate of Muhammad II. His use of Berber mercenaries caused widespread destruction in al-Andalus. His violent reign deepened the collapse of central authority.

name: Abd al-Rahman IV al-Murtada
description: Brief Umayyad claimant to the Cordoba caliphate. Raised in eastern al-Andalus against the Hammudid interlopers. Was defeated and killed after only a few months.

name: Al-Zahir — 7th Fatimid Caliph
description: Seventh Fatimid Caliph (son of al-Hakim). His reign saw a return to more moderate policies after his father's eccentricities. Began restoring religious tolerance, particularly toward Christians and Jews.

name: Abd al-Rahman V al-Mustazhir
description: Umayyad caliph in Cordoba who ruled for about a year before being assassinated. His brief reign came amid the complete disintegration of caliphal authority in al-Andalus.

name: Muhammad III al-Mustakfi
description: Another brief Umayyad claimant. Deposed and blinded after ruling for about a year. The Cordoban caliphate was by now essentially powerless and fragmented.

name: Hisham III al-Mutadd — Last Caliph of Cordoba
description: Last Umayyad Caliph of Cordoba. Deposed in 1031 when the Cordoban oligarchs abolished the caliphate. Al-Andalus then fragmented into the small Taifa kingdoms. End of the Umayyad Caliphate of Cordoba.

name: Muhammad of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Mahmud of Ghazni who briefly ruled after his father's death. Quickly overthrown by his brother Masud I.

name: Al-Qaim — 26th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-sixth Abbasid Caliph who witnessed the liberation of Baghdad from Buyid control by the Seljuk Turks in 1055. Tughril Beg, the Seljuk sultan, restored Sunni dominance and the caliphate regained some prestige.

name: Masud I of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Mahmud. Lost the decisive Battle of Dandanaqan (1040) to the Seljuk Turks, which ended Ghaznavid control of Khorasan and reduced them to a regional power in Afghanistan and Punjab.

name: Al-Mustansir — 8th Fatimid Caliph
description: Eighth Fatimid Caliph and longest-reigning caliph in Islamic history (58 years). His reign included a severe famine and political crisis in Egypt. The general Badr al-Jamali restored order and became all-powerful vizier.

name: Seljuk Empire
description: Turkic empire that ruled over a vast territory from Central Asia to Anatolia. Revived Sunni Islam under the Abbasid caliph, defeated Byzantines at Manzikert (1071), and opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement.

name: Tughril — Founder of Seljuk Empire
description: Founded the Great Seljuk Empire after defeating the Ghaznavids at Dandanaqan (1040). Entered Baghdad in 1055 and liberated the Abbasid Caliph from Buyid domination. Received the title Sultan from the Caliph — a landmark in Islamic political history.

name: Almoravid Dynasty
description: Berber Muslim dynasty from the Sahara that unified the Maghreb and Muslim Spain. Known for enforcing strict Maliki jurisprudence. Their intervention in Spain prevented Christian Reconquista advance for decades.

name: Mawdud of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Masud I who came to power by defeating his uncle Muhammad. Attempted to recover Khorasan from the Seljuks but failed. The Ghaznavid realm was now confined to Afghanistan and Sindh.

name: Yahya ibn Umar al-Lamtuni — Almoravid Commander
description: Co-founder and military leader of the Almoravid movement along with the scholar Abdullah ibn Yasin. Led the Berber Sanhaja coalition. Killed in battle against the Berghouata tribe in Morocco.

name: Masud II of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Briefly held power in Ghazni after Mawdud's death. Part of the rapid succession among Mahmud's grandsons that weakened the dynasty. Quickly removed by his uncle.

name: Ali of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Brief Ghaznavid Sultan, another grandson of Mahmud competing for the throne. Quickly displaced in the ongoing succession struggle among Sebuktigin's descendants.

name: Abd al-Rashid of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Mahmud of Ghazni who seized the throne in Ghazni for a few years. Murdered by the general Tughril who briefly usurped power before being overthrown.

name: Tughril the Usurper — Ghaznavid
description: Ghaznavid general who murdered Sultan Abd al-Rashid and briefly seized the throne in Ghazni. Killed after less than a year when the nobles rallied to restore the legitimate dynasty.

name: Farrukhzad of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Masud I who restored Ghaznavid legitimacy after Tughril's usurpation. Ruled for six years and stabilized the dynasty before his brother Ibrahim took over the sultanate.

name: Abu Bakr ibn Umar — Almoravid Commander
description: Almoravid leader who conquered Morocco and founded Marrakesh as the Almoravid capital in 1062. Returned to the Sahara to fight the Ghanaian Empire. Died in battle against the Sosso in 1087.

name: Ibrahim of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: One of the most capable later Ghaznavid sultans. Made peace with the Seljuks and concentrated on consolidating power in Afghanistan and raiding into India. A patron of Persian literature.

name: Yusuf ibn Tashfin — Almoravid Ruler
description: Greatest Almoravid ruler. Consolidated North Africa, crossed to Spain at the invitation of the Taifa kings, and defeated Alfonso VI of Castile at the Battle of Zallaqa (1086). Reunified Muslim Spain under Almoravid rule. Founded Marrakesh.

name: Alp Arslan — Seljuk Sultan
description: Son of Tughril's brother Chaghri. Defeated the Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV Diogenes at the Battle of Manzikert (1071) — a decisive victory that opened Anatolia to Turkish settlement and eventually led to the Ottoman Empire.

name: Norman Conquest of England
description: William the Conqueror of Normandy defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings and conquered England. Transformed English language, culture, law, and aristocracy. The Domesday Book (1086) was a landmark administrative achievement.

name: Malik Shah I — Seljuk Sultan
description: Son of Alp Arslan. The Seljuk Empire reached its greatest extent under his rule — from Central Asia to the Mediterranean. Patronized the great vizier Nizam al-Mulk and Omar Khayyam. The Nizamiyya universities were established in his reign.

name: Al-Muqtadi — 27th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-seventh Abbasid Caliph. Presided during the First Crusade. His reign saw the Seljuk Empire at its height under Malik Shah I. Married a Seljuk princess and exercised more influence than his recent predecessors.

name: Kilij Arslan I — Seljuk Sultan of Rum
description: Sultan of the Seljuk Sultanate of Rum (Anatolia). Defeated a People's Crusade army at Civetot (1096) but was defeated by the main First Crusade armies. Recovered and expanded Rum at the expense of Byzantines and Crusaders.

name: Al-Mustazhir — 28th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-eighth Abbasid Caliph during whose reign the First Crusade took place (1095-1099) and Jerusalem fell to the Crusaders. The Assassin order (Nizari Ismailis) was active during his caliphate.

name: Al-Mustali — 9th Fatimid Caliph
description: Ninth Fatimid Caliph whose succession over his brother Nizar caused the Ismaili split into Nizari (the Assassins) and Mustali factions. His reign saw the First Crusade take Jerusalem in 1099.

name: Berkyaruq — Seljuk Sultan
description: Son of Malik Shah I who came to power after years of dynastic civil war. His reign coincided with the First Crusade and the loss of Jerusalem. Faced constant challenges from Ismaili Assassins and rival Seljuk princes.

name: The Crusades
description: Series of religious wars by European Christians to recapture the Holy Land from Muslim rulers. First Crusade (1096-1099) captured Jerusalem. Subsequent Crusades largely failed. The last Crusader stronghold (Acre) fell in 1291. Left deep cultural and religious legacies.

name: First Crusade and Capture of Jerusalem
description: Pope Urban II called the First Crusade at Clermont. European armies marched to the Holy Land and captured Jerusalem in 1099 with mass slaughter of its Muslim and Jewish inhabitants. Established four Crusader states in the Levant.

name: Masud III of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Son of Ibrahim. Maintained the fragile Ghaznavid state. The great Persian poet Sanai wrote at his court. The dynasty continued to weaken as the Ghurids rose as rivals.

name: Al-Amir — 10th Fatimid Caliph
description: Tenth Fatimid Caliph who reasserted caliphal authority against the powerful viziers. Faced Crusader campaigns in the Levant. Assassinated by Nizari Assassins.

name: Muhammad I Tapar — Seljuk Sultan
description: Son of Malik Shah who reunified the Seljuk empire after the civil wars. Vigorously fought the Nizari Ismailis (Assassins) and the Crusaders in Syria. His reign was one of relative stability and military activity.

name: Ali ibn Yusuf — Almoravid Ruler
description: Son of Yusuf ibn Tashfin. Longest-reigning Almoravid. Faced growing Christian Reconquista pressure in Spain and the emerging Almohad challenge in Morocco. Built the famous Ben Youssef Madrasa in Marrakesh.

name: Arslan Shah of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Briefly ruled Ghazni between Shirzad and Bahram Shah. His short reign was part of the succession struggles of the later Ghaznavid period as the dynasty entered its final century.

name: Bahram Shah of Ghazni — Ghaznavid Sultan
description: Long-reigning Ghaznavid Sultan who became a vassal of the Seljuks. The Persian poet Sanai (author of Hadiqat al-Haqiqah) served at his court. The dynasty survived but in much reduced circumstances.

name: Al-Mustarshid — 29th Abbasid Caliph
description: Twenty-ninth Abbasid Caliph who tried to restore caliphal military power against the Seljuks. Was captured by the Seljuk Sultan Masud and later assassinated, possibly by Assassins.

name: Ahmad Sanjar — Last Great Seljuk Sultan
description: The last great Seljuk sultan, ruling for nearly 40 years. Dominated eastern Islamic lands from Khorasan. In 1153, the Oghuz Turks rebelled and captured him; he spent years as their prisoner. The Seljuk Empire fragmented after his death.

name: Almohad Caliphate
description: Berber Muslim caliphate that overthrew the Almoravids and ruled Morocco and Muslim Spain. At its peak, one of the largest empires in the medieval Islamic world. Declined after defeat at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212).

name: Ibn Tumart — Almohad Mahdi
description: Founder and spiritual leader of the Almohad movement. A Berber scholar who studied in the East, declared himself Mahdi, and launched a religious revolution against the Almoravids, condemning their anthropomorphic theology. His movement grew into an empire after his death.

name: Zengid Dynasty
description: Dynasty of Atabeg governors in Mosul and Syria. Unified Muslim Syria against the Crusaders and laid the groundwork for Saladin's reconquest of Jerusalem. Produced the great commander Nur al-Din Zengi.

name: Imad ad-Din Zengi — Founder of Zengid Dynasty
description: Atabeg of Mosul who unified northern Iraq and Syria. Recaptured the County of Edessa from the Crusaders in 1144 — the first major Christian loss during the Crusades. Assassinated by his own servants.

name: Al-Hafiz — 11th Fatimid Caliph
description: Eleventh Fatimid Caliph who faced severe political instability including the murder of his son by vizier Bahram. The Fatimid state began its long decline during his reign.

name: Abd al-Mumin — 1st Almohad Caliph
description: First Almohad Caliph and the true empire-builder. Conquered all of Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Spain from the Almoravids. Created one of the largest Islamic empires. The Almohad caliphate challenged both Abbasids and Fatimids.

name: Al-Rashid — 30th Abbasid Caliph
description: Thirtieth Abbasid Caliph. Deposed by the Seljuks after only one year. Was the last Abbasid caliph deposed by the Seljuks.

name: Al-Muqtafi — 31st Abbasid Caliph
description: Thirty-first Abbasid Caliph who worked to regain caliphal independence as the Seljuk Empire fragmented. Gradually extended direct caliphal control over Iraq. Repelled a Seljuk attack on Baghdad.

name: Tashfin ibn Ali — Almoravid Ruler
description: Son of Ali ibn Yusuf. Faced the Almohad rebellion which rapidly conquered Morocco. Defeated by the Almohads and killed in a fall from a cliff while fleeing. The Almoravid dynasty effectively ended with his death.

name: Ibrahim ibn Tashfin — Almoravid Ruler
description: Brief Almoravid ruler who could not hold back the Almohad advance. Reigned only months before being killed by the Almohads who captured Marrakesh.

name: Ishaq ibn Ali — Last Almoravid Ruler
description: Last Almoravid ruler. Killed when the Almohads under Abd al-Mumin captured and sacked Marrakesh in 1147. His death marked the definitive end of the Almoravid dynasty.

name: Saif al-Din Ghazi I — Zengid of Mosul
